findings.summary: ChatGPT is disclosed as an AI system in the product interface.
DALL-E/image outputs are not consistently labelled as AI-generated in ToS.
No right to explanation for automated decisions is granted in the ToS.
Gemini is disclosed as an AI assistant in the product interface.
Privacy policy references automated decisions in ad targeting but no right to contest is granted.
Google's AI principles page provides general transparency but not Art. 50-compliant disclosure per service.
Copilot and Bing Chat are labelled as AI-powered.
Responsible AI policy commits to human review for high-stakes Copilot outputs.
No per-decision explanation right is specified in the ToS.
Algorithmic feed ranking and ad targeting are automated decisions with significant effects — no right to contest is granted.
Meta previously used facial recognition for photo tagging in the EU (discontinued 2021, but infrastructure remains).
Algorithmic micro-targeting exploiting emotional states has been documented in internal Meta research.
Product recommendations and pricing are automated; no right to contest is mentioned.
Amazon Rekognition (facial recognition) is used in law enforcement contexts — Annex III high-risk.
App Store guidelines require apps using AI to disclose this. Apple Intelligence is labelled in system UI.
On-device processing and human review for App Store AI submissions are documented.
Apple provides an appeal path for automated App Store rejections.
Personalised recommendations are driven by automated profiling; ToS does not grant a right to contest.
No explicit disclosure that AI is used for content recommendation decisions.
Discover Weekly and algorithmic playlists use automated profiling; no contest right stated.
No per-feature AI disclosure as required by Art. 50 AIA.
Grok AI recommendations and feed ranking have significant effects; no right to explanation or contest.
Algorithmic amplification of emotionally provocative content has been documented; possible manipulation of behaviour.
X collects biometric data (face/voice) for premium verification — potential biometric categorisation risk.
Documented exploitation of psychological vulnerabilities (dopamine loops, infinite scroll) to maximise engagement.
For You Page is entirely algorithm-driven with significant behavioural effects; no right to contest.
Facial feature and voice data collected for effects and search — biometric categorisation risk.
My AI chatbot is disclosed as an AI system in the app.
AR lenses process biometric facial data in real time — risk of biometric categorisation.
Driver deactivation and pricing are automated decisions with significant employment effects.
AI used in employment/worker management context — Annex III high-risk category.
No right to explanation for automated driver deactivation decisions is stated in ToS.
Search ranking and host/guest matching are automated; no right to contest stated.
Personalised pricing and recommendation algorithms used; no right to contest mentioned.
Fraud detection and payment approvals are automated decisions with financial effects.
AI-based credit/payment risk decisions fall under Annex III high-risk (creditworthiness).
PayPal ToS provides a dispute process but not an explicit contest-the-algorithm right.
Post ranking and spam detection are automated; no right to contest.
No disclosure of AI usage in moderation or feed ranking.
AI moderation and spam detection have significant effects on user access; no contest right.
Clyde AI assistant was disclosed as AI but has been retired; new AI features lack explicit disclosure.
AI-generated meeting summaries and virtual backgrounds are not consistently labelled as AI-generated.
Zoom processes biometric facial data for virtual backgrounds and attention tracking.
Claude is explicitly disclosed as an AI system throughout the interface and ToS.
Usage policy and model card provide detailed transparency about Claude's capabilities and limitations.
Anthropic's responsible scaling policy commits to human oversight at every capability level.
AI persona characters may not consistently disclose they are AI — particularly concerning for minors.
Emotional attachment features targeting minors and vulnerable users may constitute exploitation of psychological vulnerabilities.
Perplexity is clearly disclosed as an AI-powered search assistant.
AI-generated answers are not consistently watermarked or labelled per Art. 50(2) requirements.
Synthesia's ToS requires users to disclose AI-generated video content but does not mandate machine-readable watermarks.
AI avatar creation from real person likeness — potential biometric categorisation from facial data.
Automated fraud detection can freeze accounts without human review — significant financial effect.
Creditworthiness and AML risk scoring are Annex III high-risk AI applications.
No right to explanation for automated account freezes or credit decisions is specified in ToS.
Credit scores are automated decisions with major legal and financial effects.
Credit scoring is explicitly listed in Annex III as a high-risk AI application.
Credit dispute process exists but is not framed as a right to contest automated AI decisions.
No meaningful explanation of scoring model is provided to the data subject.
AI-based recruitment and HR decisions (CVscreening, performance scoring) are Annex III high-risk.
Automated CV screening and promotion decisions have significant employment effects.
No right to explanation for rejected job applications processed by Workday AI.
No FRIA documentation is provided to candidates affected by Workday AI employment decisions.
AI-generated images are not machine-readable watermarked per Art. 50(2) requirements.
Midjourney discloses AI-generated nature in its terms but lacks per-image labelling.
Slack AI assistant is disclosed in the product UI.
AI-powered message classification and search ranking — no right to contest stated.
